era_context: Early Iron Age in the Mediterranean and Near East; late Twentieth Dynasty Egypt; Western Zhou in China; Vedic period in South Asia; Phoenician expansion; Aramean and Neo-Hittite kingdoms consolidating; Greece in the early Iron Age (Geometric period).

events.title: Phoenician maritime expansion and trade network development
events.description: Phoenician city-states, particularly Tyre and Sidon, were expanding their maritime trade networks throughout the Mediterranean and beyond during the early Iron Age. By the 11th century BCE, Phoenician merchants were establishing trading posts and colonies across the Mediterranean, including in North Africa, Cyprus, and eventually the western Mediterranean. This expansion was driven by the decline of Egyptian and Hittite naval power and the demand for Phoenician cedar, dyes, and manufactured goods. The year 1032 BCE falls within this period of accelerating expansion.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Phoenician sites (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos) and colonial settlements
Egyptian and Assyrian records mentioning Phoenician trade
Later Greek sources (Herodotus, Strabo) on Phoenician colonization

events.certainty_note: Phoenician expansion is archaeologically attested and confirmed by later sources. However, precise dating of specific trading posts and voyages to individual years is not possible; 1032 BCE is assigned as representative of the 11th-century expansion process.

events.title: Vedic period consolidation in South Asia — early Rigvedic culture
events.region: South Asia (Indus Valley and Gangetic plains)
events.description: The Vedic period in South Asia (c. 1500–500 BCE) was underway, with Indo-Aryan speaking peoples establishing pastoral and agricultural communities across northern India. The Rigveda, the oldest of the Vedic texts, was being composed orally during this era (though not written down until much later). By 1032 BCE, Vedic culture was becoming increasingly established, with the development of ritual practices, social hierarchies (including early forms of the varna system), and agricultural settlements. This period laid the foundation for later Vedic civilization and eventually Hinduism.

sources.name: Rigveda (oral composition, later written down; traditional dating c. 1500–1200 BCE)
Archaeological evidence from sites such as Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and Vedic settlement sites

events.certainty_note: The Vedic period is archaeologically attested, but precise dating is uncertain by ±100–200 years. The Rigveda's composition date is traditionally placed c. 1500–1200 BCE, but some scholars propose later dates (c. 1200–900 BCE). The year 1032 BCE is assigned as a representative point within this broader period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Phoenician expansion has been revised by modern scholarship; some scholars argue that major colonial expansion occurred later (9th–8th centuries BCE) rather than the 11th century. The traditional narrative of Phoenician dominance of Mediterranean trade may overstate their role relative to other maritime peoples. The Rigveda's composition date is highly contested; some scholars place it as late as 900 BCE, which would shift the 'Vedic period' chronology significantly. The notion of a unified 'Vedic culture' may be an oversimplification of a more diverse, regionally varied set of Indo-Aryan societies.
historiographic_note: For Phoenician history, the archaeological record is primary; written sources are sparse and often from rival or later civilizations. For the Vedic period, the Rigveda is the main textual source, but it is an oral composition with uncertain dating and mythological content mixed with historical references. Egyptian and Assyrian records provide external corroboration for some Phoenician activities but are limited in scope. The archaeological record for South Asia is rich but interpretation is contested, particularly regarding the relationship between Indus Valley Civilization and Vedic peoples.

graph_edges.note: Early Phoenician expansion in the 11th century led to the establishment of major colonies and trading networks by the 8th century BCE.
The Vedic period consolidation continued until the rise of the Mahajanapadas and the Buddha in the 6th–5th centuries BCE.
